Okay, so I'm gonna pretend like I'm you and it's gonna be fun, but I gotta figure out if you've got anything for that. Where's my fat marker? There you go. Okay, I got a lump like that. How do you do it? The right leg or the left leg? Left leg. All right, I'll get it. All right, so what I'm gonna do, cut this in half. Here is what you're gonna get. Okay, all right, so I'm gonna interview you so I can understand this. So tell me about yourself. About myself or about the product? You first. All right, so I'm Ron Wilson. I'm from South Carolina. So that's the family business, doing the lawnmowers, doing the irrigation, all kind of stuff. And then you got back in computer science. Yeah, background in computer science. I can tell you why I got into this world. And this doesn't really apply to our customer, but about two years ago, two and a half years ago, this Jacobson manufacturer, they're about 80% of our revenue. We've got around 40 employees. I think we've got like three, four, five buildings, 20 something acres. It's not as big as a lot of people's companies. There's a lot of stuff moving with our parent company. Two years ago, we thought the line was going to be taken away from us. So myself, my wife, my mom, my dad, our director of sales, kind of the management team. We were the only ones that knew this. We spent the next like 48 hours going through how in the world can we keep these doors open and came up to if this happened, the doors were going to have to shut. We were going to have to have 40 families that. Sorry guys. You don't know where the check is going to come from. So fortunately for us, that didn't happen. We maintained the exclusive rights and I looked at my parents and I said, this will never, ever happen again. We will never be in this position to where somebody holds the power over us. So I started figuring out how can I do that? Well, process software, knowing the golf market. This is what I've come up with is this software. So the last two years I've been building it, you guys have seen me limp and I've told my story quite a bit, but shattered my like obliterated my ankle on a trampoline with my then three year old. So I've been building the software for two years. I've had four surgeries, two surgeons actually after the third surgery didn't work, recommended amputation. I ended up with a surgeon in Charlotte that does a lot of the Carolina Panthers work. He's fixed my leg. It's still not perfect, but at least I saved my leg. My wife has gone through a pregnancy, a delivery and raising a 15 month old that's never seen me walk. I've walked, I put on 40 pounds. Started walking about three or four months ago over the last two years. So my entrepreneurial journey that I thought was going to be easy, like a lot of life smacks in the face. Every time I feel like I get to the top of the mountain, I look, it's just another cliff, right? I've had a lot more rock to climb, but do feel like we're getting close. So you jump on a trampoline, how, tell me about that. So I don't know. I mean, at that point I was, I was 200 pounds. I was in pretty good shape. And my three year old fell underneath me as I was up in the air and I just kind of twisted out of the way. So I didn't land on him and then just exploded. Doctor said it was like the equivalent of a 75 mile an hour head on collision. Wow. If they were to ampute it would have been. It was going to be but beneath the knee. I'd already like I had already come to terms with the amputation. I was so tired of not being able to walk, not being able to be a father. I had to stop coaching. And so I was ready to do it, but I wanted to have a lasted, you know, a couple of opinions and they finally found somebody said they could fix it and they fixed it. That's cool. So. All right. So. Come by your friend yesterday or tell me about your friend that got you into the golf. Yeah. So the Chuck story is true. His name is actually Chuck. The sequence of events didn't happen the way my origin story was written yesterday. But in the 90s, there's big boom in golf construction. Golf course is being built left and right. We had just economic supply demand over supply, but the demand was still there. So I think this Tiger Woods is awesome. Everyone be Tiger Woods. Dude, you'd be. Yeah. I mean, that has a lot to do with it. So then, you know, 2008 happened, Tiger Woods happened, all these different things happened that just made golf start to constrict. I was having a conversation with, I'm not sure. Anyways, today with somebody about, you know, you probably don't play golf. I don't play golf. I bet the majority of us in here don't play golf. Who plays golf? Are you memberships? So that's less than like a quarter of the room. You're probably public, semi-private play. You're probably not a member, right? You've not given that capital contribution. You're not maintaining that monthly minimum. So revenues, capital expenditures, everything has shrunk for golf. And so golf is a shrinking economy. Our customer Chuck went to school to learn the craft of grass, how to make that disease go away, how to make it look as healthy and as green as possible. But there was a pretty major transition in the golf market to where budgets were starting to be tied. And Chuck used to have all the budget that he ever needed to do whatever he wanted. He was a rock star at his course. Members loved him. He went out eating, drinking with the members, playing with the members. Then when they started shrinking his budget, conditions started going down. Chuck started feeling the heat. Chuck knew how to grow grass. He didn't know how to run the business side, what I call the business. He understood the craft, not the business of running a $2 million budget. So we saw this. So this is kind of where the timeline changes a little bit with what actually happened. We saw this was a problem. We actually tried to find some software to help the literal Chuck out and then all the other Chucks that are in the industry. And the fact of the matter is it didn't exist. There was no software out there for this kind of new day and age of the golf economy. So fast forward a year and a half ago, we're building the software. Chuck gets fired from his where he was a rock star. He's gone. Families upset, uprooted. They're looking at moving halfway around the country for like a 50% pay decrease. Just it's not good. And Chuck's not the only story of this happening. So we're developing this software and we give the software to Chuck and some other Chucks and it's not good. So I never had proper training in like running a software development team. So I've spent the last two years and amongst all the other stuff kind of getting what we call my MBA in software development and like running a software team. So through this process, we realized that it's a lot more than just the software that Chuck needed. My favorite kind of like bridge is it's kind of like giving Chuck an easel and a paintbrush, but never teaching him how to paint. So the software is the easel and the paintbrush. So on my way out here, it was an epiphany I had a week ago. On my way out here, we started building out, okay, what does this class look like to teach Chuck how to paint? So that's kind of where we are right now. I've got my staff is in the process of building out all these assets. We're going to do a master class for Chuck and all the Chucks out there. And then our software is just a tool to implement that. Is it marketing software or is it? No, it's mainly, so you know, as being a business owner, I guess we all do, our biggest expense is labor, is payroll. That is the same for golf as well. Chuck's responsible for a $2 million budget and labor, equipment, chemical and fertilizer are the three biggest labor equipment fertilizer, labor, equipment, and then the chemicals and fertilizers to keep the grass healthy are the three biggest line items for a golf course is overall budget. So maintenance is the biggest line item for a golf course is budget. And then in the maintenance budget, those are the three big ones. So Chuck is not very good at managing people. Chuck is not very good at managing processes. So our system, we call it plan execute report.
